summary: McKenzie describes adding a 'Wizards menu' feature to Bingo Card Creator to simplify the user experience of accessing pre-loaded word lists. The original four-step file navigation process was too complex for his non-technical target users. He built the feature in about an hour late at night, using a Factory pattern for extensibility. The post illustrates his philosophy of reducing friction for end users and designing for future feature expansion from the start.
key_points: Four steps to open a file is trivial for programmers but a significant barrier for non-technical users
Good OO design with extensibility in mind enables rapid feature development comparable to frameworks like Rails
Investing small amounts of extra time in patterns like Factory classes pays off by enabling future features without refactoring
New features should be evaluated not just for current utility but as potential market expansions (e.g., math problem generators)
money_quotes: Sure, four steps is nothing for a computer programmer, but my users… aren't.
If you start with extensibility and code reuse in mind, Java or any other OO language can be used to rapidly code in new features almost as quickly as Rails or the other new hotness these days.
If you don't, you end up with hundreds of classes full of totally unmaintainable spaghetti code.
Why code a factory when I could have just subclassed JMenuItem for a single purpose? Because with a Factory I can decide tomorrow to make another type of wizard.
Hey, wait, I think there could be a market for that one…
key_insight: Small investments in extensible design and user-experience simplification compound into significant product advantages over time.
